answer:
**In the Bṛhaspati Smṛti, 'lekhya'—translated here as "document" or "written document"—refers to a formal written instrument serving as legal proof in disputes, particularly those involving debts, property transactions, partitions, gifts, sales, pledges, and related matters.**[^1][^2] It is one of three primary kinds of *human proof* (alongside witnesses and possession/inference), distinct from *divine proof* (ordeals).[^3] Bṛhaspati dedicates section 1.6 to its rules, defining a valid document (*lekha*) as one recording the custom of the country, date (year, month, etc.), interest (if applicable), and signatures of the debtor, witnesses, and scribe.[^4] Documents come in types like royal charters, partition deeds, gifts, sales, mortgages, compacts, bonds of servitude, loans, and victory documents (recording court decisions).[^5] They must avoid flaws like forgery, improper witnesses/scribes, poor condition, or coercion.[^6]

**'Lekhya' differs from *sakṣya* ("testimony," from witnesses) as one form of proof yields to another based on availability and doubt:** Human proofs are prioritized over divine ones; if a document exists, neither witnesses nor ordeals are needed.[^7] Doubt in a document (or witness testimony) requires an ordeal for clarification.[^8] Witnesses (*sakṣin*)—detailed in section 1.5—are oral testifiers (up to 12 kinds, e.g., subscribed, concealed, kinsmen), unsuitable if wicked (thieves, etc.).[^9] Thus, *lekhya* is fixed, enduring written evidence (surviving dead witnesses/time), while *sakṣya* is living, spoken evidence, potentially conflicting or forgettable.

The term *lekhya* itself appears in related Smṛtis (e.g., Viṣṇu Smṛti 7.1), but Bṛhaspati's focus aligns terminologically and substantively.[^10]

[^1]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-510297).

[^2]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-c9cb41).

[^3]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-44a57a).

[^4]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-dc2d5a).

[^5]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-f172b3) (intro to documents); rules elaborated in subsequent paragraphs (e.g., p-213342 onward).

[^6]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-bbce9d).

[^7]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-44a57a).

[^8]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-b5ea9a).

[^9]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-5a2654).

[^10]: The exact Sanskrit *lekhya*/*lekha* is not rendered in this translation but contextually matches; cf. Viṣṇu Smṛti, Chapter 7, [paragraph](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/07/translation/#p-510625).
